text: I’d love to chat on blade design. I am a hydraulic engineer and I do work with fan blades as well. I’d try a big 3 blade or a curved cup. Layered thin wood. Glued and twisted w routed leading edges. On a gyro w spring loaded cooper connections so it won’t tangle the wire. A fin on the back to orient it to the wind. And a tear drip shaped cover for the motor.

author: @davidprocter3578
text: I have a memory of an Irish company back in the seventies who produced a bearing less turntable for a record player, using the current to create an electromagnetic field that levitated and trapped the turntable and spun it at the required speed. Obviously this idea would not work for a wind generator as it stands ? but if you had two or three discs of magnets opposing one another a similar effect could be induced?? to provide a friction less bearing, quite obviously they would need to be trapped together. I suppose I aught to say I live in a very low wind area the average wind speed is 3 miles an hour and with a build height restriction of 2.5 meters before requiring planning permission. a horizontal wind generator would appear to be my only option to provide the required current to power my home. So you can see my need for a low impact/cost wind turbine with sufficient output to meet the needs of the average household, I don't imagine that this could be done without a battery bank. Also 45 years ago I lived near a inventor with similar problems he built a horizontal turbine in his back garden which powered his house it was quite large and took up much of his back garden, we lived then in a very high average wind speed area but his mill was located between the rear of his house and a steep hillside rising behind his home, however even on seemingly windless days the thing turned. I am afraid he kept its internal working secret but the turbine itself was probably 12 feet in diameter with approx four foot square wind apertures much like a van top fan sat on a three foot plinth no idea what was used for a bearing but rather suspect it was a rear axle from a car or van considering the weight of the fan itself made from angel iron and galvanized sheet steel. Any thoughts?

author: @matthewnicholson1560
text: I can see a possible upgrade to the system. The reason it is reasonably efficient is that the cone concentrates the pressure on small blades. As the air passes down the sides of the cone the pressure increases and hits small blades that have a low pressure behind them. This pressure differential allows the small blades to work in a high pressure differential whereas most conventional wind turbines work with large blades in a lower pressure differential. The problem that remains is that the air is then travelling in the wrong direction because it wants to escape radially rather than go through the gap between the blades. By putting a funnel shaped flange on the outer edge of the blades that radial escape route is not available and the air is more inclined to escape through the blades. The spiral on the cone is probably a mistake because that will cause the air to spin with the cone. Energy will be lost by causing the air to spin as it enters the blades, because all you are doing is creating additional turbulence that will heat up the air but not be converted into electricity. So keep it simple but add a funnel shaped flange should increase the efficiency.

author: @stephanverbeeck
text:
🤓🧠⚡⚡⚡I just got a GREAT idea from watching your videos.  Consider this:
1) The central cone increases the amount of air flowing over the spoon blades but it would also do that if it was NOT part of the rotating part.
2) So that implies that it is NOT required that ALL of the blade surface is spinning.
3) 90% of the blade can be fixed guiding the airflow over the 10% of the blade that actually rotates
4) round things are hard to fabricate while using a centrifugal fan in combination with a much larger bend rectangular plate to guide the wind into it are easy to make.
5) the fixed part of the blade can be mounted on the CORNER of an existing wall or TOP of the gable roof of your house increasing the used surface manifold while it costing noting and being visually pleasing
6) using the wind that is pushed AROUND your house is much cheaper than having to put the turbine on a very high pole or tower.  Like that you also do not disturb your neighbors with a high construction.  Just think how large your roof is and how much air it can push toward/into your centrifugal turbine like that

I would love to see you try something like that because:
1) it is really easy and really cheap
2) it would have far more energy than any free standing centrifugal fan type of generator as those feel the wind at 2 sides of the generator axis while the fixed front panel takes the negative energy away and feeds it onto the positive side.

What do you think?
If it works than it might be both the best and cheapest design you have ever build and the generator would also work better if the wind direction is not 100% correct by the fixed blade correcting the direction partly.  The fixed part only has to be really smooth (like the wing of a glider plane)

The challenge is of course to find which surface and curvature+angle is optimal

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think it would workout my question right back at you is - why don't you build it? It sounds like a good plan and I would like to see it too - but I would more like to see it if you gave it a go

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Without a 3D printer, plastic can be heated and reformed to some degree. It doesn't always mean starting from scratch when minor angle adjustments can be made by adding heat. If strengthening is required add some 2 part epoxy glue to the back making sure that you maintain balance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good points mate - thanks for adding that

author: @dotoid
text: The EU actually banned single-use plastic cutlery in 2021. So it might not be that easy to get hold of anymore.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I went into my local store a week ago - there was loads - it might be the ban is in cafes only just right now - there was certainly no shortage for me and a quick look on amazon will reveal a lot !!

replies.author: @dotoid
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering UK is not in EU anymore. :)

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @dotoid The UK really wasn't a member of the EU, to begin with, it cost them 10 billion pounds per year and the insistent EU law & regulation adding cost them far more than that the whole thing was a half baked fish pie a week old.

author: @jasonnewby4908
text: I wonder how beneficial it would be to create a shroud for the face of the wind wall that would help funnel air to the turbines 🤔

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you could give it a go on a small scale mate - say about 4 in a square? See what you get

replies.author: @hommerdalor6301
replies.text:
Yes, with helicoidal blades fixed on the shroud coming up close to the turbine cone, inducing rotation pressure/higher air speed, and the same idea to create rotating aspiration on the back side, and maybe adjacent turbines counterrotating to limit, or increase turbulences (at your best convenance ;-) ) 
Cheers.

replies.author: @plainedgedsaw1694
replies.text:
@AORD72 because building a 100m across shroud 100m above ground and keeping it from shattering during storms would be pretty complicated task i think.
But at near ground square windwall it should be child's play.

replies.author: @plainedgedsaw1694
replies.text: @AORD72 watch his videos then, he said he achieved 90+% of betz limit. He is not trying to make the best turbine for ideal conditions, but a way to extract some usable energy in an urban environment.

replies.author: @plainedgedsaw1694
replies.text: @AORD72 60W actually wouldn't be a bad result in my opinion.
